summary:
There is no direct evidence from the retrieved literature showing that bicompartmental knee arthroplasty provides better膝 muscle strength or physical performance compared to total knee arthroplasty; all related studies focus on unilateral replacements or theoretical gains without head-to-head comparisons. Thus, based on available data, it cannot be concluded that bicompartmental surgery is more favorable.  

\boxed{\text{C}}

Final summary only as requested – exactly TWO SENTENCES focusing solely on KEY CLINICAL POINTS RELEVANT TO THE QUESTION.

Key point: The comparison lacks empirical support regarding muscle strength/physical performance. Clinical relevance lies in functional outcome differences post-surgery—not just symptom relief.

Revised concise version meeting requirements precisely:

No retrievals directly compare bilateral compartment involvement versus full joint replacement concerning muscular strength or activity levels. Consequently, neither procedure has been proven clinically superior in restoring dynamic knee functionality through enhanced neuromuscular output.
